one_liner: A 28-year-old female with a history of pelvic inflammatory disease presents with sudden-onset left lower quadrant pain, vaginal bleeding, and dizziness.

stage2.hpi_extension: Patient reports sudden onset of sharp, left lower quadrant abdominal pain that began approximately 2 hours ago. The pain is constant, rated 8/10, and radiates to her left shoulder. She has associated vaginal spotting (dark brown blood) that started this morning. She feels lightheaded, especially when standing, and had one episode of near-syncope. Her last menstrual period was 7 weeks ago, and her cycles are usually regular (28-day cycle). She is sexually active with one male partner and uses condoms inconsistently. Home pregnancy test was positive 5 days ago but she has not yet seen an OB provider. She denies fever, nausea, vomiting, dysuria, or prior similar episodes.
stage2.pmh: Pelvic inflammatory disease (treated 2 years ago with antibiotics), chlamydia infection (age 24), no chronic medical conditions
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker. Occasional alcohol (stopped after positive pregnancy test). Works as a teacher. No illicit drug use.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Pale, diaphoretic young woman in moderate distress. HEENT: Pale conjunctivae. Dry mucous membranes. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. S1/S2 normal. No murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, tender in the left lower quadrant with voluntary guarding. Mild rebound tenderness. No rigidity. Normoactive bowel sounds. Pelvic exam: Scant dark blood in vaginal vault. Closed cervical os. Left adnexal tenderness with a palpable, tender fullness. Cervical motion tenderness present. Extremities: No edema. Cool extremities. Capillary refill 3 seconds.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Above discriminatory zone (~1500-3000) - intrauterine pregnancy should be visible on TVUS if present
Low hematocrit consistent with blood loss
Low progesterone (<5 ng/mL) suggests non-viable pregnancy

imaging_results.result: Empty uterus with thickened endometrium. Left adnexal mass (3.2 cm) with a hyperechoic ring sign. Moderate free fluid in the cul-de-sac.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: No intrauterine pregnancy despite hCG above discriminatory zone. Adnexal mass and free fluid highly suggestive of ruptured this condition.

imaging_results.result: Free fluid in the pelvis (Morison pouch negative, pelvis positive).
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Confirms hemoperitoneum in the setting of hemodynamic instability

stage5.final_clue: Quantitative beta-hCG of 3,200 mIU/mL with no intrauterine pregnancy on transvaginal ultrasound, a left adnexal mass with ring sign, free pelvic fluid, and hemodynamic instability confirm a ruptured this condition requiring emergent surgical intervention.

debrief.classic_presentation: A woman of reproductive age with amenorrhea (6-8 weeks), unilateral lower abdominal/pelvic pain, and vaginal bleeding. Risk factors include prior PID, ectopic pregnancy, tubal surgery, IUD use, and assisted reproduction. Rupture presents with acute hemodynamic instability, peritoneal signs, and referred shoulder pain (from diaphragmatic irritation).
debrief.pathophysiology: Ectopic pregnancy occurs when a fertilized ovum implants outside the uterine cavity, most commonly in the fallopian tube (95%). Risk factors that damage tubal epithelium (PID, prior surgery, smoking) impair ovum transport. As the embryo grows, it outstrips the tubal blood supply and distends the tube, eventually causing rupture with life-threatening hemorrhage into the peritoneal cavity.
debrief.treatment_overview: Ruptured ectopic with hemodynamic instability requires emergent surgical intervention (usually laparoscopic salpingectomy). Simultaneously resuscitate with IV crystalloid and blood products. If Rh-negative, administer RhoGAM (300 mcg IM). Unruptured ectopic may be managed with methotrexate (if criteria met: hemodynamically stable, hCG <5000, no fetal cardiac activity, mass <3.5 cm) or expectant management (if hCG declining). Serial hCG monitoring until undetectable regardless of treatment.
high_yield_pearls: Any woman of reproductive age with abdominal pain must have a pregnancy test performed.
The discriminatory zone for TVUS is beta-hCG ~1,500-3,000 mIU/mL; absence of IUP above this level suggests ectopic.
Shoulder pain in a pregnant woman with abdominal pain suggests hemoperitoneum with diaphragmatic irritation.
RhoGAM must be given to all Rh-negative patients with any pregnancy-related bleeding.
Heterotopic pregnancy (concurrent IUP and ectopic) occurs in ~1/30,000 natural conceptions but up to 1/100 with assisted reproduction.
sources: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 193: Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy (2018)
